planting.note: Plant bare-root trees in early spring in well-drained, fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; Shiro is a Luther Burbank introduction from 1899 and remains one of the most widely grown Japanese plums in American home orchards — a vigorous, very productive yellow-fruited variety with a reliably sweet, mild flavor and early ripening that makes it one of the first summer stone fruits to harvest.

directions: Plant in well-drained, fertile, slightly acidic soil in full sun; requires a pollinator — plant with Santa Rosa, Methley, Beauty, or another Japanese plum for best fruit set. Shiro plum was introduced in 1899 by Luther Burbank, America's preeminent fruit breeder, as part of his extensive Japanese plum improvement program; it remains one of the most popular and widely grown Japanese plums more than 125 years after its introduction, prized for very early ripening (late June to July), consistent heavy production, and large, round, golden-yellow fruits with thin, transparent yellow skin and sweet, mild, honey-flavored yellow flesh. Chill requirement is approximately 400–500 hours, making it suitable for zones 5–9 but less reliable in the lowest-chill Gulf Coast areas. The large crops ripen quickly and have short shelf life; fruit should be harvested when skin turns fully yellow and fruit gives to gentle pressure. An excellent pollinator for other Japanese plum varieties.
sources: UC Davis Department of Plant Sciences
Virginia Cooperative Extension Fruit Culture Guide

planting.note: Plant bare-root trees in early spring in well-drained, fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; Satsuma is an early 20th century Japanese plum introduction with one of the lowest chill requirements of any high-quality Japanese plum (approximately 200–300 hours), making it one of the best plum varieties for Gulf Coast zone 8 and mild California coastal gardens.

directions: Plant in well-drained, fertile, slightly acidic soil in full sun; Satsuma cross-pollinates best with Santa Rosa, Beauty, Methley, or other Japanese plums. Satsuma is a distinctive Japanese plum selection of early 20th century origin whose most striking characteristic is its blood-red to deep crimson-purple interior flesh — unlike most plums with yellow or amber flesh, Satsuma's flesh is the same deep red as the skin, producing richly pigmented juice and a distinctive wine-like, full-flavored sweetness with more depth and richness than most yellow-fleshed Japanese plums. The medium-large, round fruits have deep red to maroon-black skin; they ripen in late July to August. With only approximately 200–300 chill hours required, Satsuma is one of the most reliably productive Japanese plums for Gulf Coast zone 8 and similar low-chill climates. The deep red flesh makes spectacular jam, preserves, and fresh eating; the color intensifies when cooked. Outstanding for Gulf Coast, Texas, and Central and Southern California gardens where insufficient chill makes most European plums unproductive.

planting.note: Plant bare-root trees in early spring in well-drained, fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; Superior is a University of Minnesota 1957 release combining Japanese and American plum genetics for outstanding cold hardiness and excellent fruit quality — one of the best plum varieties for zones 4–6 where most Japanese plums winter-kill or fail to fruit reliably.

directions: Plant in well-drained, fertile, slightly acidic soil in full sun; Superior is partially self-fruitful but produces heavier crops cross-pollinated with Toka, Pipestone, or another Minnesota plum hybrid. Superior plum was released in 1957 by the University of Minnesota fruit breeding program, which developed a series of plum hybrids combining Japanese plum (Prunus salicina) quality and fruit size with the extreme cold hardiness of native American plum species (Prunus americana); these hybrids fill an important niche for gardeners in zones 4–6 where winters regularly kill Japanese plums or prevent adequate chill hours for European varieties. Superior produces large (2–3 inch), dark red-maroon, round fruits with yellow flesh and excellent sweet-tart flavor — one of the largest and best-flavored of the Minnesota hybrids and clearly superior in fruit quality to earlier hybrid selections. Very cold-hardy (surviving to –40°F) and blooms late enough to avoid most late spring frosts. A large, vigorous, productive tree suited to the Upper Midwest and Northern Plains where most stone fruits struggle.
sources: University of Minnesota Extension Fruit Growing Guide

planting.note: Plant bare-root trees in early spring in well-drained, fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; Elephant Heart is a Luther Burbank introduction from around 1929 and one of the most distinctive and impressive Japanese plums for fresh eating — very large, dark, blood-fleshed fruits with an exceptionally rich, sweet-tart, wine-like flavor that sets it apart from more mildly flavored modern varieties.

directions: Plant in well-drained, fertile, slightly acidic soil in full sun; requires cross-pollination with Santa Rosa, Shiro, Satsuma, or another Japanese plum. Elephant Heart is a Luther Burbank plum introduced around 1929 and named for the large, heart-shaped fruits that can weigh 4–6 oz each — unusually large even among Japanese plums. The dark red to near-black skin encloses blood-red to deep crimson flesh with intensely rich, sweet-tart, wine-complex flavor that many consider the most interesting and complex of the Japanese plum flavors; it is notably more flavorful and less mild than Shiro or Santa Rosa when eaten at peak ripeness (late August to September, relatively late for Japanese plums). The depth of pigmentation (both in skin and flesh) makes Elephant Heart particularly striking and flavorful for fresh eating, jam, and preserves. Chill requirement is approximately 400–500 hours. The distinctive, intense flavor comes through even when cooked; a justly famous heirloom plum maintained by Slow Food and heirloom orchardists.
sources: UC Davis Department of Plant Sciences
Slow Food USA Ark of Taste

planting.note: Plant bare-root trees in early spring in well-drained, fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; Kelsey is one of the earliest Japanese plums introduced to North America, brought from Japan in 1870 by John Kelsey of Berkeley, California and valued for its very large, dramatically shaped, green-yellow fruits and excellent heat tolerance that suits Gulf Coast and Southern California gardens well.

directions: Plant in well-drained, fertile, slightly acidic soil in full sun; Kelsey cross-pollinates with Santa Rosa, Shiro, Satsuma, and most other Japanese plums. Kelsey is historically significant as one of the first Japanese plums introduced to North America, brought from Japan in 1870 by John Kelsey of Berkeley, California and widely distributed through the California nursery trade in the late 19th century, where it contributed to Luther Burbank's subsequent Japanese plum breeding work. The very large (3–5 oz), heart-shaped to irregular, somewhat flattened fruits have distinctive mottled green and yellow skin with a red blush on the sun-exposed cheek, ripening to golden-yellow with a sweet, mild, honeyed yellow flesh. Kelsey is a late-season variety (August–September) with lower chill requirements than many Japanese plums (approximately 300–400 hours), making it well-suited to Gulf Coast zone 8 and mild California gardens; the flesh firms up during ripening, giving the fruit good keeping quality and making it one of the better shipping and storage plums for home orchardists.
sources: UC Davis Department of Plant Sciences
